one_line_thesis: Global QSR franchisor (KFC, Taco Bell, Pizza Hut, Habit); watch at ~$165 on 3.52% synthesis IRR — quality franchise cash at a full price with leverage.

deep_dive.executive_summary:
Yum! Brands is a global QSR franchisor: $68.3 billion of FY2025 system sales across KFC, Taco Bell, Pizza Hut, and Habit, with 97% franchised units and $1.64 billion of FY2025 free cash flow (~$5.83 per share). Same-store sales were +3% in FY2025; system sales grew +4% reported. Taco Bell ($18.4 billion system sales, 9,030 units) and KFC ($36.4 billion, 33,897 units) carry the portfolio; Pizza Hut ($12.8 billion, 19,974 units) remains the relative laggard.

At about $165.13 per share (Yahoo Finance, 2026-07-10), the Lawrence base case implies about 3.52% per year on normalized owner cash of...

decision.next_action: Freeze the evidence packet and select three method-independent raters.

valuation.interpretation: value_per_share is withheld while any material additive component is unpriced; priced_components_per_share is not a complete security value.

scenario_contract.rule: Cases must change cited causal operating, capital, probability, timing, or financing drivers—not only a terminal multiple.

change_control.rule: A source fact is locked. Every assumption change requires a reason, author, timestamp, and before/after value.

optionality.rule: Every option requires a beneficiary, probability, timing, remaining capital, failure case, and non-overlap control; absence of option value must be explicit rather than unvalued.

committee.next_action: Freeze the evidence packet and select three method-independent raters.

method_fit.label: High-return compounder
applicability_tests: Owner earnings can be separated from stock compensation, working capital, and maintenance capital.
Incremental returns on retained capital are observable and durable.
The competitive advantage explains pricing, retention, share, or cost outcomes.
Terminal value is supported by a reinvestment runway rather than an unexplained multiple.
failure_modes: growth projected without its capital cost
terminal value unsupported by a durable mechanism

validation_cohort.purpose: Test depletion, commodity exposure, finite reserves, and royalty-unit reconciliation.
Test tangible capital, credit losses, funding, regulation, and excess-capital realization.
Test owner earnings, capital-light reinvestment, cyclicality, and buyback value creation.
Test normalized margins, industry supply response, replacement cost, and reinvestment timing.
Test product concentration, pipeline probabilities, patent cliffs, and catalyst timing.

method_fit.rule: Use one primary method and no more than two corroborating methods; irrelevant methods and personas remain silent.

outcomes.weighting_rule: Do not change a persona's relevance within a power zone until at least 20 attributable, dividend-aware outcomes exist there; never reweight automatically.

attribution.explanation: A complete security value is intentionally withheld while material components are unpriced. Attribution restarts after the first proof-complete baseline.

total_return_panel.error: Vendor events are discovery evidence; primary-source completeness has not been established.
total_return_panel.return_contract: split-adjusted close excluding dividends; event amounts normalized for later splits; cash reinvested at ex-date close; pre-tax nominal

coverage.note: Vendor events are discovery evidence; primary-source completeness has not been established.

timeline.label: Total Assets moved +21.9% versus the prior period (6,727.0 to 8,197.0).
Q1 2026: revenues $2.06B (+15% YoY); diluted EPS $1.55 (+72% YoY); operating profit $644M
FY2025: revenues $8.21B (+8.8%); system sales $68.3B (+4%); same-store sales +3%; OCF $2.01B; repurchased $552M; dividend $2.84/sh
Scott Mezvinsky named CEO, KFC Division (from Pizza Hut CEO role)
Acquired KFC U.K. and Ireland stores from franchisee (store acquisition)
Accelerated digital/AI platform (Byte by Yum!) and global franchise development post-pandemic normalization
Suspended KFC/Pizza Hut operations in Russia; re-franchising and brand exit costs
Acquired Habit Burger & Grill (~300 units) for ~$375M to enter fast-casual burger segment
Spun off Yum China (YUMC) as independent listed company; YUM retained ex-China KFC, Taco Bell, Pizza Hut
Completed separation from Yum! Restaurants International structure; pure-play franchisor model accelerated
Spun off from PepsiCo; became independent public company Yum! Brands

industry.structure: Global quick-service restaurant oligopoly at the brand level: Yum! operates four concepts (KFC, Taco Bell, Pizza Hut, Habit) across 155 countries with 63,285 units, 97% franchised. Revenue is primarily franchise royalties and fees on $68.3B system sales, not store-level revenue.
industry.share_shift: Taco Bell gaining U.S. traffic and average check; KFC growing internationally (99% franchised, 90% ex-U.S. units); Pizza Hut losing share to delivery-native pizza competitors in key markets.
industry.trend: Franchisors investing in digital ordering, AI-assisted operations, and asset-light unit growth; consumer value sensitivity and labor inflation pressure franchisee margins while brand-level fee streams remain resilient.

risk_claims.claim: *Primary risk: Pizza Hut continues losing share to delivery-native competitors, triggering franchisee closures and royalty pressure faster than KFC/Taco Bell growth can offset. [Fact] Pizza Hut system sales $12.8 billion on 19,974 units versus Taco Bell $18.4 billion on only 9,030 units implies weaker unit productivity
Leverage: $11.9 billion long-term debt constrains buybacks in a rate or earnings shock (10-K FY2025).
Habit drag: Company-owned Habit units (79% of U.S. Habit) with minimal division profit (~$13 million operating profit on $706 million system sales) (10-K segment note).
